standard_description: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Below is a square pyramid and a square prism. Which has the larger volume? Explain your reasoning.
###IMAGE0###

Task
My sister’s birthday is in a few weeks and I would like to buy her a new vase to keep fresh flowers in her house. She often forgets to water her flowers and needs a vase that holds a lot of water. In a catalog there are three vases available and I want to purchase the one that holds the most water. The first vase is a cylinder with diameter 10 cm and height 40 cm. The second vase is a cone with base diameter 16 cm and height 45 cm. The third vase is a sphere with diameter 18 cm.
###IMAGE0###
Which vase should I purchase?
How much more water does the largest vase hold than the smallest vase?
Suppose the diameter of each vase decreases by 2 cm. Which vase would hold the most water?
The vase company designs a new vase that is shaped like a cylinder on bottom and a cone on top. The catalog states that the width is 12 cm and the total height is 42 cm. What would the height of the cylinder part have to be in order for the total volume to be $1224\pi \text{ cm}^3$?
###IMAGE1###
Design your own vase with composite shapes, determine the volume, and write an ad for the catalog.

Problem 1
Watch this video demonstration:
Cylinder, Cone, and Sphere Volume
. (Note, the beginning of the video reviews the volume of a cone; the demonstration of a sphere's volume starts at 1:30.)
a.   What is the relationship between the volume of a cylinder and the volume of a sphere with the same diameter and height?
b.   A sphere is enclosed in a cylinder. The diameter of the sphere is equal to the height of the cylinder.
###IMAGE0###
What is the height of the cylinder above, in terms of
$$r$$
?
What is the formula for the volume of the cylinder above, in terms of
$$r$$
?
What is the formula for the volume of the sphere inside the cylinder?
Problem 2
A cylinder has a diameter of
$${16}$$
inches and a height of
$${14}$$
inches. What is the volume of the largest sphere that will fit into the cylinder?
###IMAGE1###
Problem 3
The volume of an inflated beach ball is
$${288\pi \space \mathrm{cm}^3}$$
. What is the radius of the ball?
###IMAGE2###

Problem 1
What are all the two-dimensional shapes that can result from slicing a rectangular prism?
What are all the two-dimensional shapes that can result from slicing a rectangular pyramid?
How are the slices of a rectangular prism similar to and different from the slices of a rectangular pyramid?
Problem 2
Below is a diagram of a right rectangular pyramid that is intersected by a plane parallel to the base. The base dimensions are 4 inches by 6 inches. The height of the pyramid is 10 inches, and the plane intersects the pyramid at 5 inches from the base.
###IMAGE0###
What is the relationship between the area of the base and the area of the cross-section?
What is the relationship between the volume of the top part of the pyramid, above the plane creating the cross-section, and the full pyramid?
Problem 3
Determine the volume of the truncated cone shown below.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
What is the relationship between the following three volumes?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Below is a soap bubble on a table with a diameter of 8 centimeters. What is the volume of the air inside the bubble? Assume the soap bubble itself has no thickness.
###IMAGE1###
Problem 3
How many paper balls will fit in the can?
###IMAGE2###

Problem 1
Look at the four figures shown below.
###IMAGE0###
a.   What do you notice about each figure? What do you wonder about each figure?
b.   What measurements would you need in each figure to determine its volume?
Problem 2
Option #1:
Glasses
The diagram shows three glasses (not drawn to scale). The measurements are all in centimeters.
###IMAGE1###
The bowl of glass 1 is cylindrical. The inside is 5 cm and the inside height is 6 cm.
The bowl of glass 2 is composed of a hemisphere attached to a cylinder. The inside diameter of both the hemisphere and the cylinder is 6 cm. The height of the cylinder is 3 cm.
The bowl of glass 3 is an inverted cone. The inside diameter is 6 cm and the inside slant height is 6 cm.
Calculate the volume of the bowl of each of these glasses.
Glass 2 is filled with water and then half the water is poured out. Find the height of the water.
Problem 3
Option #2
:
Shipping Rolled Oats
Rolled oats (dry oatmeal) come in cylindrical containers with a diameter of
$$5$$
inches and a height of
$${9 {1\over2}}$$
inches. These containers are shipped to grocery stores in boxes. Each shipping box contains six rolled oats containers. The shipping company is trying to figure out the dimensions of the box for shipping the rolled oats containers that will use the least amount of cardboard. They are only considering boxes that are rectangular prisms so that they are easy to stack.
a.   What is the surface area of the box needed to ship these containers to the grocery store that uses the least amount of cardboard?
b.   What is the volume of this box?
Problem 4
Option #3: Comparing Snow Cones
###IMAGE2###
Pablo's Icy Treat Stand sells homemade frozen juice treats as well as snow cones. Originally, Pablo used paper cone cups with a diameter of 3.5 inches and a height of 4 inches.
His supply store stopped carrying these paper cones, so he had to start using more standard paper cups. These are truncated cones (cones with the "pointy end" sliced off) with a top diameter of 3.5 inches, a bottom diameter of 2.5 inches, and a height of 4 inches.
Because some customers said they missed the old cones, Pablo put a sign up saying, "The new cups hold 50% more!" His daughter Letitia wonders if her father's sign is correct. Help her find out.
How much juice can cup A hold? (While cups for juice are not usually filled to the top, we can assume frozen juice treats would be filled to the top of the cup.)
How much juice can cup B hold?
By what percentage is cup B larger in volume than cup A?
Snow cones have ice filling the cup as well as a hemisphere of ice sticking out of the top of each cup. How much ice is in a snow cone for each cup?
By what percentage is the snow cone in cup B larger than the snow cone in conical cup A?
Is Pablo’s sign accurate?
Problem 5
Option #4: Flower Vases
My sister’s birthday is in a few weeks and I would like to buy her a new vase to keep fresh flowers in her house. She often forgets to water her flowers and needs a vase that holds a lot of water. In a catalog, there are three vases available and I want to purchase the one that holds the most water. The first vase is a cylinder with diameter 10cm and height 40cm. The second vase is a cone with base diameter 16cm and height 45cm. The third vase is a sphere with diameter 18cm.
###IMAGE3###
Which vase should I purchase?
How much more water does the largest vase hold than the smallest vase?
Suppose the diameter of each vase decreases by 2cm. Which vase would hold the most water?
The vase company designs a new vase that is shaped like a cylinder on the bottom and a cone on top. The catalog states that the width is 12cm and the total height is 42cm. What would the height of the cylinder part have to be in order for the total volume to be 1,224 π cm
3
?
Design your own vase with composite shapes, determine the volume, and write an ad for the catalog.
Problem 6
Option #5
:
Tennis & Golf Balls
###IMAGE4###
Tennis balls are packaged into cylindrical cans to ship and sell. Three tennis balls fit into one cylinder so that the height of the three balls is equal to the height of the cylinder, and the diameter of one ball is equal to the diameter of the cylinder.
The radius of one tennis ball is
$${1.3}$$
inches. How much air space is in the cylinder around the tennis balls?
Golf balls are sometimes packaged into square prisms to ship and sell. Three golf balls fit into one square prism, similar to how the tennis balls fit into the cylinder.
What is the formula for the volume of the square prism? Give your answer in terms of
$$d$$
, the diameter of the golf ball.
The volume of the square prism is
$${192 \space \mathrm{cm}^3}$$
. What is the diameter of one golf ball?
How much air space is in the square prism around the golf balls?
Problem 7
Choose a problem that you did not solve already and review the work of your peers on their poster.
a.   Briefly describe their approach to the problem.
b.   Would you have taken a similar approach? Why or why not?
c.   Do you agree with their solution?

Problem 1
The cylinder is full of water. The water flows out through a pipe at the bottom of the cylinder. Imagine looking down on the cylinder as the water flows out of it. Draw the shape of the surface of the water at five different levels.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Sketch the cross sections of the coffee cup at each of the heights shown below.
###IMAGE1###
Problem 3
Given the following cross-sections, equilateral triangles from the same three-dimensional figure:
###IMAGE2###
Sketch the figure from which the cross-sections were taken.
If the height of the three-dimensional figure is 10 feet, what is the volume of this three-dimensional figure?

Problem 1
Find the volume of the right prism and right pyramid below. How do the volumes compare?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
A cylindrical glass with a height of 10 centimeters and a radius of 3 centimeters is filled with lemonade. How many cone-shaped cups with half the height of the glass and the same radius can be completely filled with the lemonade from the glass?

Task
Pablo's Icy Treat Stand sells home-made frozen juice treats  as well as snow-cones.  Originally, Pablo used paper cone cups with a diameter of 3.5 inches and a height of 4 inches.
###IMAGE0###
Conical Cup A
His supply store stopped carrying these paper cones, so he had to start using more standard paper cups.  These are truncated cones (cones with the "pointy end" sliced off) with a top diameter of 3.5 inches, a bottom diameter of 2.5 inches, and a height of 4 inches.
###IMAGE1###
Cup B
Because some customers said they missed the old cones, Pablo put a sign up saying "The new cups hold $50\%$ more!"  His daughter Letitia wonders if her father's sign is correct.  Help her find out.
###IMAGE2###
How much juice can cup A hold? (While cups for juice are not usually filled to the top, we can assume frozen juice treats would be filled to the top of the cup.)
How much juice can cup B hold?
By what percentage is cup B larger in volume than cup A?
Snow cones have ice filling the cup as well as a hemisphere of ice sticking out of the top of each cup. How much ice is in a snow cone for each cup?
By what percentage is the snow cone in cup B larger than the snow cone in conical cup A?
Is Pablo's sign accurate?
You are designing a square prism that has a height of 4 inches. What are two possible base side lengths if you want a minimum volume of 24 cubic inches and a maximum volume of 35 cubic inches?

Snow globes consist of a glass sphere that is filled with liquid and other contents. If the inside radius of the snow globe is 3 inches, find the approximate volume of material in cubic inches that can fit inside.
###IMAGE0###

Task
Rolled oats (dry oatmeal) come in cylindrical containers with a diameter of 5 inches and a height of 9$\frac12$ inches. These containers are shipped to grocery stores in boxes. Each shipping box contains six rolled oats containers. The shipping company is trying to figure out the dimensions of the box for shipping the rolled oats containers that will use the least amount of cardboard. They are only considering boxes that are rectangular prisms so that they are easy to stack.
What is the surface area of the box needed to ship these containers to the grocery store that uses the least amount of cardboard?
What is the volume of this box?

Problem 1
Figure A shows the water level before the rock is put in the water. Figure B shows the water level after the rock is put in the water. What is the volume of the rock?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Below is a two-dimensional pattern that will be spun on an axis to create a three-dimensional shape.
###IMAGE1###
Find the volume if this two-dimensional pattern is spun on the
$$x$$
-axis.
Find the volume if this two-dimensional pattern is spun on the
$$y$$
-axis.
What is the difference in volume between spinning the figure on the
$$x$$
-axis and spinning the figure on the
$$y$$
-axis?
Problem 3
Given the right square prism below:
###IMAGE2###
How is the volume affected when the height is doubles?
How is the volume affected when the side of the square is doubled?

Task
The diagram shows three glasses (not drawn to scale). The measurements are all in centimeters.
###IMAGE0###
The bowl of glass 1 is cylindrical. The inside diameter is 5 cm and the inside height is 6 cm.
The bowl of glass 2 is composed of a hemisphere attached to cylinder. The inside diameter of both the hemisphere and the cylinder is 6 cm. The height of the cylinder is 3 cm.
The bowl of glass 3 is an inverted cone. The inside diameter is 6 cm and the inside slant height is 6 cm.
Find the vertical height of the bowl of glass 3.
Calculate the volume of the bowl of each of these glasses.
Glass 2 is filled with water and then half the water is poured out. Find the height of the water.
[Adapted with permission from the Mathematics Assessment Project.]

At the beginning of the summer, Alessandra bought a new piece of chalk, shown below. She used it for several weeks until it was almost gone. What was left of the chalk is also shown below. All measurements are given in inches, and the figures are not drawn to scale.
How much chalk did Alessandra use? Give your answer in cubic inches.
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Use a piece of 8.5"
$$\times$$
11" paper to create a baseless rectangular prism as shown below.
###IMAGE0###
Find the length, width, and height of the baseless prism.
State the formula for finding the volume of the prism and calculate the volume.
What do you notice about the length and width? Rewrite the formula with only two variables to reflect this observation.
Problem 2
A container holds a maximum of 120 cubic inches of water. A cylindrical pitcher has a height of 4 inches. You want to transfer the water from the cylindrical pitcher to the container. What is the maximum radius of the pitcher, to the nearest tenth, that can be used to fill the container without it overflowing?
Problem 3
A can of soda is split between two glasses. If you want the most soda, which glass would you choose? Explain your reasoning.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
A pentagonal prism and a cylinder are shown below. Use the measurements given to determine the volume of each figure.
###IMAGE0###
Area of pentagonal base:
$$27\space \mathrm{u}^2$$
Height of prism:
$$6.2 \space \mathrm{u}$$
Volume: __________
Area of circular base:
$$16\space \mathrm{u}^2$$
Height of cylinder:
$$5.8 \space \mathrm{u}$$
Volume: __________
Problem 2
a.   A cylindrical can of cranberry sauce measures
$$3$$
inches in diameter and
$${4{7\over{16}}}$$
inches in height. What is the volume of the can? Give your answer to the nearest tenth of a cubic inch.
b.   A different brand of cranberry sauce also comes in a cylindrical can and has a volume of
$$50\space\mathrm{in}^3$$
. The can has a height of
$${5.5}$$
inches. What is the radius of the can? Give your answer to the nearest tenth of an inch.
Problem 3
Watch this video,
"How Many Cones Does it Take to Fill a Cylinder with the Same Base and Height"
.
a.   What is the relationship between the volume of a cylinder and the volume of a cone with the same base and height?
b.   The cylinder and cone below have congruent bases and the same height.
###IMAGE1###
If the volume of the cylinder is
$${90\space \mathrm{cm}^3}$$
, what is the volume of the cone?
If the volume of the cone is
$${120\space \mathrm{cm}^3}$$
, what is the volume of the cylinder?
If the volume of the cylinder is given by the formula
$${V=\pi r^2h}$$
, what is the formula for the volume of the cone?

In pottery class, Asher and Brandi make three-dimensional solid shapes out of clay.
a.   Asher makes a cylinder with a radius of
$$3$$
inches and a height of
$${6 {1\over2}}$$
inches. How many cubic inches of clay did Asher use?
b.   Brandi makes a cone and uses approximately
$$64 \space \mathrm{in}^3$$
of clay. The height of Brandi’s cone is
$$4$$
inches. What is the radius of the circular base of Brandi’s cone?

Problem 1
A right circular cylinder with a radius of 1 centimeter has been cut from the center of the right rectangular prism below. Find the volume.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Two cylindrical containers have the same capacity. A designer wants to increase the volume so that each container could still hold the same amount. He doubles the height of one container and doubles the radius of the other. Will scaling each container as described result in the same volume? Why or why not?

Problem 1
Below is a mockup of an aerial view of the proposed Apple headquarters. Twelve thousand people are proposed to work at the headquarters.
###IMAGE0###
What is the population density when all the employees are at the headquarters?
Problem 2
A cylindrical soda can is made of aluminum. It is approximately
$$4\frac{3}{4}$$
inches high and the top and the bottom has a radius of approximately
$$1\frac{3}{16}$$
inches.
###IMAGE1###
Find the approximate surface area of the soda can. What assumptions do you use in your estimate?
The density of aluminum is approximately 2.70 grams per cubic centimeter. If the mass of the soda can is approximately 15 grams, how many cubic centimeters of aluminum does it contain?
Use the answers to parts (a) and (b) to estimate how thick the aluminum can is.

Problem 1
Three tennis balls are in a can, each with a diameter of 2.6 inches.
###IMAGE0###
How much felt is used to cover the three tennis balls?
What is the minimum surface area of the can, including the top and bottom?
Problem 2
The following four packages all contain the same amount of candy. Rank the packages based on the least amount of packaging used to the most amount of packaging used.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
For each of the following cross-sections, sketch two possible figures from which the cross-sections were taken.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Horizontal slices of a solid are shown at various levels arranged from highest to lowest. What could the solid be?

$$y$$
-axis?
###IMAGE0###

A gigantic key lime pie was made and served to Bostonians in South Station. The pie was cylindrical with a diameter of 9 feet, weighed about one thousand pounds, and was about 8 inches tall. If 2,000 people were served, how many pounds was each person given? What was the fraction of the gigantic pie that each person received?

Below is a truncated cone with height and two radii marked.
###IMAGE0###
Write an expression that can be used to determine the volume of the truncated cone. Explain what each part of the expression represents.
Find the volume of the truncated cone.

A standard soccer ball measures
$${22 \space \mathrm{cm}}$$
in diameter.
###IMAGE0###
What is the volume of a standard soccer ball? Give your answer to the nearest whole cubic centimeter.

khan_exercises.title: Apply volume of solids
khan_exercises.description: Solve problems concerning real-world situations with the volumes of cones, cylinders, pyramids, prisms, and spheres.
topic_path: Module 7: Introduction to irrational numbers using geometry
Topic D: Applications of radicals and roots

items.question_content:
A round [[☃ definition 1]] has a diameter of $115\text{ cm}$ and is $25\text{ cm}$ deep. A hose is filling the pool at a rate of $34{,}000\text{ cm}^3$ per minute. 

**How long will it take to fill the pool to a depth of $20\text{ cm}$?**  
*Round to the nearest minute.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a cylinder to represent the water in the wading pool.



[[☃ image 1]]

We don't need the full depth of the pool, because we're modeling the water, which is only $\blueE{20\text{ cm}}$ deep. 

We need to find the volume of the water, then find out how many minutes it takes to get that volume of water from the hose.

###Volume of a cylinder

The diameter is $115\text{ cm}$, so the radius is $\maroonD{57.5\text{ cm}}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= \pi \cdot (\text{radius length})^2 \cdot (\text{height}) \\\\
&= \pi \cdot \maroonD{57.5}^2 \cdot (\blueE{20})\\\\
&= 66{,}125 \pi\\\\
&\approx \purpleD{207{,}738}
\end{align}$

When the water is $\blueE{20\text{ cm}}$ deep, it will have a volume of about $\purpleD{207{,}738\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Filling rate

A hose is filling the pool at a rate of $\goldE{34{,}000\text{ cm}^3}$ per minute.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{filled}} &= (\text{filling rate}) (\text{time})\\\\
\purpleD{207{,}738} &\approx \goldE{34{,}000} \redE{t}\\\\
\dfrac{\purpleD{207{,}738}}{\goldE{34{,}000}} &\approx \redE{t}\\\\
6.1&\approx \redE{t}
\end{align}$
To the nearest minute, it takes $\redE{6}$ minutes to fill the pool to a depth of $20\text{ cm}$.

options.definition: a shallow artificial pool for children

items.question_content:
Jesse is filling spherical balloons. When full, one of these balloons has a diameter of $24\text{ cm}$. Jesse can fill a balloon at a rate of $820\text{ cm}^3$ per breath. 

**How many breaths does it take Jesse to fill a balloon?**  
*Round to a whole breath.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] breaths
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a sphere to model a balloon.



[[☃ image 1]]

We need to find the volume of the balloon, then find out how many breaths it takes Jesse to fill that volume.

###Volume of a sphere

The diameter is $24\text{ cm}$, so the radius is $\maroonD{12\text{ cm}}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{sphere}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\text{radius length})^3 \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{full}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\maroonD{12})^3\\\\
&\approx \purpleD{7{,}238}
\end{align}$

A fully-inflated balloon has a volume of about $\purpleD{7{,}238\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Filling rate

Jesse can fill a balloon at a rate of $\goldE{820\text{ cm}^3}$ per breath. 

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{filled}} &= (\text{filling rate}) (\text{\# of breaths})\\\\
\purpleD{7{,}238} &\approx \goldE{820} \redE{n}\\\\
\dfrac{\purpleD{7{,}238}}{\goldE{820}} &\approx \redE{n}\\\\
8.8&\approx \redE{n}
\end{align}$
In whole breaths, it takes Jesse $\redE{9}$ breaths to fill a balloon.

items.question_content:
Terry is filling spherical water balloons with a faucet that puts out $100\text{ cm}^3$ of water per second. At that rate, it takes Terry $6$ seconds to fill a balloon.

**What is the radius of a filled balloon?**  
*Round to the nearest centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a sphere to model a balloon.



[[☃ image 1]]

We need to find the volume of a full balloon, and then we can solve for its radius.

###Volume

We're told that it takes Terry $6$ seconds to fill a balloon at a rate of $100\text{ cm}^3$ of water per second. Let's use that to find the volume of a filled balloon.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{filled}} &= (\text{filling rate}) (\text{time})\\\\
&= (100)(6)\\\\
&=\purpleD{600}
\end{align}$

A filled balloon has a volume of $\purpleD{600\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Radius

Now we can solve for the radius of a sphere whose volume is $\purpleD{600\text{ cm}^3}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{sphere}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\text{radius length})^3 \\\\
\purpleD{600} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (r)^3\\\\
\dfrac{3}{4\pi} \cdot \purpleD{600}&= \dfrac{\cancel{4}}{\cancel{3}}\cancel{\pi} (r)^3 \cdot \dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{4\pi}}\\\\
\sqrt[3]{143.2} &\approx \sqrt[3]{r^3}\\\\
5.2 &\approx r
\end{align}$

A full balloon has a radius of about $5.2\text{ cm}$.
To the nearest centimeter, the radius of a full balloon is $5\text{ cm}$.

items.question_content:
Luis has a pyramid shaped plant pot. It has a square base with a side length of $36\text{ cm}$, and the height of the pot is $36\text{ cm}$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Luis wants to fill the pot with soil so that the soil takes up $75\%$ of the pot's volume.

**How far up the pot will the soil reach?**  
*Round to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a pyramid to model the pot and the soil that fills it, whose volume is $75\%$ of the pot (diagram not to scale).



[[☃ image 1]]

There are two ways we could find how far up the pot the soil will reach:

1. Find the scale factor of dilation from the pyramid that models the pot to the pyramid that models the soil, then scale the height.
- Find the volume of the pot, scale it to find the volume of the soil, then calculate the height.

Let's go with the second strategy, where we find the volume of the pot, and the volume of the soil.

###Volume of the pot

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{pyramid}} &= \dfrac{1}{3} (\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{pot}} &= \dfrac{1}{3}(36 \cdot 36)(36)\\\\
&= \maroonD{15{,}552}
\end{align}$

The pot has a volume of $\maroonD{15{,}552\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Volume of the soil

The soil needs to have $75\%$ the volume of the pot.

$0.75 \cdot \maroonD{15{,}552} = \purpleD{11{,}664}$

The soil has a volume of $\purpleD{11{,}664\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Solve for height

For the pot, the edge length of the base is equal to the height. Since the pyramids are similar, this will also be true in the pyramid that models the soil. Let's call the edge length of the base and the height $\ell$ in the pyramid that models the soil.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{pyramid}} &= \dfrac13(\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
\purpleD{11{,}664}&= \dfrac13 \cdot (\ell)^2\cdot (\ell) \\\\
34{,}992&= \ell^3\\\\
\sqrt[3]{34{,}992}&=\sqrt[3]{\ell^3}\\\\
32.71 &\approx \ell
\end{align}$

The soil will reach about $32.7\text{ cm}$ up the pot.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: An upside down square-based pyramid with a base length of thirty-six centimeters and a height of thirty-six centimeters.

items.question_content:
A company makes spherical tanks that store chemicals. Their standard model has a diameter of $6$ meters, but a customer needs a larger tank whose capacity is $2.5$ times the volume of the standard model.

**What is the approximate diameter of this larger tank?**  
*Round to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a sphere to model the standard tank and the larger tank, whose capacity is $2.5$ times the volume of the standard model.


[[☃ image 1]]

We could find the diameter of the larger tank two ways:

1. Find the scale factor of dilation from the standard tank to the larger tank, then scale the diameter.
2. Find the volume of the standard tank, scale it to find the volume of the larger tank, then calculate the radius and diameter lengths.

Let's go with the second strategy, where we find the volume of each tank.

###Standard tank volume

The standard tank has a diameter of $6\text{ m}$, so it has a radius of $\blueE{3\text{ m}}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{sphere}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\text{radius length})^3 \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{standard}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\blueE{3})^3\\\\
&= \maroonD{36\pi}
\end{align}$

The standard tank has a volume of $\maroonD{36\pi\text{ m}^3}$.

###Larger tank volume

The larger tank needs to have $2.5$ times the volume of the standard tank.

$2.5 \cdot \maroonD{36\pi} = \purpleD{90\pi}$

The larger tank has a volume of $\purpleD{90 \pi \text{ m}^3}$.

###Solve for radius & diameter

Now we can use the formula for volume to solve for the radius of the larger tank.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{sphere}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\text{radius length})^3 \\\\
\purpleD{\text{volume}_{\text{large}}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (r)^3\\\\
\purpleD{90 \pi} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (r)^3\\\\
\dfrac{3}{4\cancel{\pi}} \cdot \purpleD{90 \cancel{\pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{4}}{\cancel{3}}\cancel{\pi} (r)^3 \cdot \dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{4\pi}}\\\\
\sqrt[3]{67.5} &=\sqrt[3]{r^3}\\\\
4.07 &\approx r
\end{align}$

Since the radius of the larger tank is about $4.07\text{ m}$, the diameter is about $8.14\text{ m}$.

To the nearest tenth of a meter, that's $8.1\text{ m}$.

The larger tank has a diameter of about $8.1\text{ m}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

items.question_content:
Wesley makes spherical fish tanks with a diameter of $30\text{ cm}$. A customer requests a smaller tank with half the volume of the tank that Wesley usually makes.

**What is the approximate diameter of the smaller tank?**  
*Round to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a sphere to model the standard tank and the smaller tank, whose volume is half of the standard tank.


[[☃ image 1]]

We could find the diameter of the smaller tank two ways:

1. Find the scale factor of dilation from the standard tank to the smaller tank, then scale the diameter.
- Find the volume of the standard tank, scale it to find the volume of the smaller tank, then calculate the radius and diameter lengths.

Let's go with the second strategy, where we find the volume of each tank.

###Standard tank volume

Wesley makes tanks with a diameter of $30\text{ cm}$, so each tank has a radius of $\blueE{15\text{ cm}}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{sphere}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\text{radius length})^3 \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{standard}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\blueE{15})^3\\\\
&= \maroonD{4{,}500\pi}
\end{align}$

The standard tank that Wesley makes has  a volume of $\maroonD{4{,}500\pi\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Smaller tank volume

The smaller tank needs to have half the volume of the standard tank.

$\dfrac12 \cdot \maroonD{4{,}500\pi} = \purpleD{2,250\pi}$

The smaller tank has a volume of about $\purpleD{2{,}250\pi\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Solve for radius & diameter

Now we can use the formula for volume to solve for the radius of the smaller tank.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{sphere}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (\text{radius length})^3 \\\\
\purpleD{\text{volume}_{\text{small}}} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (r)^3\\\\
\purpleD{2250 \pi} &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (r)^3\\\\
\dfrac{3}{4\cancel{\pi}} \cdot \purpleD{2250 \cancel{\pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{4}}{\cancel{3}}\cancel{\pi} (r)^3 \cdot \dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{4\pi}}\\\\
\sqrt[3]{1687.5} &=\sqrt[3]{r^3}\\\\
11.906 &\approx r
\end{align}$

Since the radius of the smaller tank is about $11.906\text{ cm}$, the diameter is about $23.812\text{ cm}$.

To the nearest tenth of a centimeter, that's $23.8\text{ cm}$.

The smaller tank has a diameter of about $23.8\text{ cm}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

items.question_content:
Amira has a pyramid shaped plant pot. It has a square base with a side length of $30\text{ cm}$, and the height of the pot is $30\text{ cm}$. 


[[☃ image 1]]

Amira wants to fill the pot to $\dfrac12$ of its height with soil.

**What is the approximate volume of soil that Amira needs?**  
*Round to the nearest cubic centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}^3$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

If we fill the pyramid pot to $\dfrac12$ of its height, the soil takes the form a smaller pyramid that's similar to the pot.

Since the pyramids are similar, we can set up a proportional equation to find the side lengths and height of the smaller pyramid, and then find its volume.

###Volume of soil

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{pyramid}} &= \dfrac13(\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac13 \cdot (\goldE{\ell})^2\cdot (\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac13 \cdot \goldE{15}^2\cdot(15)\\\\
&= 1125
\end{align}$
Amira needs $1125\text{ cm}^3$ of soil.

options.alt: An upside down square-based pyramid with a base length of thirty centimeters and a height of thirty centimeters.

items.question_content:
Anju's office uses paper cups shaped like cones to serve water. Each cup has a maximum diameter of $8\text{ cm}$ and a height of $10\text{ cm}$. Anju fills the cup to $\dfrac34$ of its height to prevent spills.

**What is the approximate volume of water in Anju's cup?**  
*Round to the nearest cubic centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}^3$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a cone to model the cup.



[[☃ image 1]]

If we fill the cup to $\dfrac34$ of its height, the water takes the form of a cone that is similar to the cup.

Since the cones are similar, we can set up a proportional equation to find the radius and height of the water, and then find its volume.

###Radius and height of water

The cup is filled to $\dfrac34$ of its height of $10\text{ cm}$, which is $7.5\text{ cm}$:



[[☃ image 1]]

We can solve for the radius of the water in the cup by using a proportional equation.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\blueE{r}}{4} &= \dfrac{7.5}{10}
\\\\
\blueE{r} &= \dfrac{7.5}{10} \cdot 4
\\\\
\blueE{r} &= \blueE{3}
\end{align}$

###Volume of water

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cone}} &= \dfrac13(\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac13\cdot \pi \cdot (\text{radius length})^2\cdot (\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac13\cdot  \pi \cdot \blueE{3}^2\cdot(7.5)\\\\
&= 22.5\pi\\\\
&\approx \purpleD{70.69}
\end{align}$
To the nearest cubic centimeter, the volume of water in the cup is about $71\text{ cm}^3$.

items.question_content:
Sanjay makes souvenir pyramids by pouring liquid into a  pyramid-shaped mold. The mold he uses has a square base with a side length of $10\text{ cm}$, and the height of the mold is $10\text{ cm}$. 



[[☃ image 1]]

Sanjay wants to make a smaller pyramid using the same mold, so he plans to fill the mold $2\text{ cm}$ from the top.

**What is the approximate volume of this smaller pyramid?**  
*Round to the nearest cubic centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}^3$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

If we fill the pyramid mold $2\text{ cm}$ from the top, we have a smaller pyramid that's similar to the original pyramid.

Since the pyramids are similar, we can set up a proportional equation to find the side lengths and height of the smaller pyramid, and then find its volume.

###Volume of smaller pyramid

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{pyramid}} &= \dfrac13(\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac13 \cdot (\blueE{\ell})^2\cdot (\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac13 \cdot \blueE{8}^2\cdot(8)\\\\
&= \dfrac{512}{3}=170.\overline{6}\\\\
&\approx \purpleD{170.67}
\end{align}$
To the nearest cubic centimeter, the volume of the smaller pyramid is about $171\text{ cm}^3$.

options.alt: An upside down square-based pyramid with a base length of ten centimeters and a height of ten centimeters.

items.question_content:
Alandra's rectangular cake pan is $33\text{ cm}$ by $23\text{ cm}$. She has enough batter to fill it to a depth of $3\text{ cm}$. Instead, Alandra wants to pour the batter into ice cream cones. She plans to fill each cone to a depth of $9\text{ cm}$ with a diameter of $4\text{ cm}$.

**How many whole cones can Alandra fill?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] whole cones
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a rectangular prism to model the amount of cake batter in the rectangular pan. 



[[☃ image 1]]

Let's use a cone to model the amount of cake batter in each cone.


[[☃ image 2]]

The number of cones we use, multiplied by the volume of each cone, should be less than or equal to the amount of cake batter. We are looking for the largest number of cones we can use under this restriction.

###Volume of a prism

The volume of the cake batter equals the amount of space it takes up in the rectangular pan.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{prism}} &= (\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= (33\cdot 23)(3) \\\\
&= \blueE{2{,}277}
\end{align}$

The cake batter has a volume of $\blueE{2{,}277\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Volume of a cone

The diameter is $4\text{ cm}$, so the radius is $\maroonD{2\text{ cm}}$. The batter in each cone has a depth of $9\text{ cm}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cone}} &= \dfrac{1}{3} (\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{3} (\pi \cdot \maroonD{2}^2) (9) \\\\
&=  \goldE{12\pi}\\\\
&\approx \goldE{37.7}
\end{align}$

Alandra can pour about $\goldE{37.7 \text{ cm}^3}$ of batter into each cone.

###Number of cones

As mentioned above, assuming we use $n$ cones, then $n\cdot 
\goldE{37.7}$ must be less than or equal to $\blueE{2{,}277}$. Let's solve this inequality:

$\begin{align}
n\cdot \goldE{37.7}&\leq\blueE{2{,}277}
\\\\
n&\leq\dfrac{\blueE{2{,}277}}{\goldE{37.7}} \approx 60.4
\end{align}$

Since we only want completely filled cones, we can only count $60$ of the cones.
Alandra can fill $60$ whole cones.

items.question_content:
Sharon is making a large batch of soup. The soup reaches a height of $25\text{ cm}$ in a cylindrical pot whose diameter is $30\text{ cm}$. To store the soup for later, she'll pour it into ice cube molds where each cube has edges that are $7\text{ cm}$ long.

**How many whole cubes can Sharon make?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] whole cubes
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a cylinder to represent the soup in the pot. We don't need the full depth of the pot, because we're modeling the soup, and it reaches a height of $\blueE{25\text{ cm}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Let's use a cube to model one of the ice cubes.



[[☃ image 2]]

The number of cubes we make, multiplied by the volume of each cube, should be less than or equal to the amount of soup in the pot. We are looking for the largest number of cubes we can use under this restriction.

###Volume of a cylinder

The volume of the soup equals the amount of space it takes up in the cylindrical pot. The diameter of the pot is $30\text{ cm}$, so the radius is $\maroonD{15\text{ cm}}$. Also, the soup reaches a height of $\blueE{25\text{ cm}}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= \left(\pi r^2\right)(h) \\\\
&= \left(\pi \cdot \maroonD{15}^2\right)(\blueE{25}) \\\\
&= \purpleD{5{,}625\pi}
\end{align}$

The soup has a volume of $\purpleD{5{,}625\pi\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Volume of a cube

Each cube has edges that are $7\text{ cm}$ long.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cube}} &= (\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
&= (7 \cdot 7) (7) \\\\
&\approx \goldE{343}
\end{align}$

Each cube contains $\goldE{343 \text{ cm}^3}$ of soup.

###Number of cubes

As mentioned above, assuming we make $n$ cubes, then $n\cdot 
\goldE{343}$ must be less than or equal to $\purpleD{5{,}625\pi}$. Let's solve this inequality:

$\begin{align}
n\cdot \goldE{343}&\leq\purpleD{5{,}625\pi}
\\\\
n&\leq\dfrac{\purpleD{5{,}625\pi}}{\goldE{343}} \approx 51.5
\end{align}$

Since we only want whole cubes, we can only count $51$ of the cubes.
Sharon can make $51$ cubes.

items.question_content:
An ice cream company sells their product in a cylindrical container that is $16\text{ cm}$ tall and has a diameter of $14\text{ cm}$. They are considering switching to a new container that is a rectangular prism whose base is $20 \times 13 \text{ cm}$.

The company wants the new container to have the same volume as the old container.

**How tall should the new container be?**  
*Round to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Modeling the situation

Let's use a cylinder to represent the old container.



[[☃ image 1]]

Let's use a rectangular prism to model the new container.


[[☃ image 2]]

Once we know the volume of the old container, we can use it to find height the new container.

###Volume of a cylinder

The diameter is $14\text{ cm}$, so the radius is $\maroonD{7\text{ cm}}$. The height of the container is $\blueE{16\text{ cm}}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\text{base area})(\text{height}) \\\\
&= \left(\pi r^2\right)(h) \\\\
&= \left(\pi \cdot \maroonD{7}^2\right)(\blueE{16}) \\\\
&= \purpleD{784\pi}
\end{align}$

The old container has a volume of $\purpleD{784\pi\text{ cm}^3}$.

###Volume and height

The new container is a rectangular prism whose base is $20 \times 13 \text{ cm}$ and also has a volume of $\purpleD{784\pi\text{ cm}^3}$. We can use this to solve for the height.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{\text{volume}_{\text{prism}}} &= (\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\purpleD{784\pi} &= (20 \cdot 13) (h) \\\\
\purpleD{784\pi} &= 260h \\\\
h &= \dfrac{\purpleD{784\pi}}{260} \\\\
h &\approx 9.47
\end{align}$
The new container should be about $9.5\text{ cm}$ tall.

items.question_content:
A company makes cylindrical crackers that have a cylindrical hole in the middle. 



[[☃ image 1]]

The diameter of the entire cracker is $16\text{ mm}$, the diameter of the hole is $8\text{ mm}$, and the cracker is $18\text{ mm}$ long.

**What is the volume of the material used in each cracker?**  
*Round to the nearest cubic millimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{mm}^3$
hints:
###Strategy

We can find the volume of the material used in the cracker by finding the volume of the entire snack as if there wasn't a hole, and then subtracting the volume of the hole.

$\blueE{\text{volume}_{\text{material}}} = \maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire}}} - \goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{hole}}}$

###Entire volume

The entire snack is a cylinder with a diameter of $16\text{ mm}$, so its radius is $8\text{ mm}$. Its length (or height) is $18\text{ mm}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\pi r^2) (h) \\\\
\maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire}}}  &= (\pi \cdot 8^2) (18) \\\\
\maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire}}}  &=  \maroonD{1{,}152\pi}
\end{align}$

The entire snack has a volume of $\maroonD{1{,}152\pi \text{ mm}^3}$ (let's not round this until the end).

###Volume of hole

The hole is a cylinder with a diameter of $8\text{ mm}$, so its radius is $4\text{ mm}$. Its length (or height) is $18\text{ mm}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{cylinder}} &= (\pi r^2) (h) \\\\
\goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{hole}}}  &= (\pi \cdot 4^2) (18) \\\\
\goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{hole}}}  &=  \goldE{288\pi} 
\end{align}$

The hole has a volume of $\goldE{288\pi \text{ mm}^3}$ (let's not round this until the end).

###Material volume

$\begin{align}
\blueE{\text{volume}_{\text{material}}} &= \maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire}}} - \goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{hole}}} \\\\
&= \maroonD{1{,}152\pi} - \goldE{288\pi} \\\\
&= \blueE{864\pi} \\\\
&\approx \blueE{2{,}714.3}
\end{align}$
The volume of the material used in each cracker is about $\blueE{2714\text{ mm}^3}$.

options.alt: A large cylinder with a smaller cylindrical hole removed from the center. The diameter of the big cylinder is sixteen millimeters, the diameter of the smaller cylinder is eight millimeters. The height of both cylinders is eighteen millimeters.

items.question_content:
A company makes a greenhouse that is shaped like a rectangular prism on bottom and a half cylinder on top.



[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangular prism has a base that is $3 \times 9\text{ m}$ and a height of $2\text{ m}$.

**What is the volume of this greenhouse?**  
*Round to the nearest cubic meter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}^3$
hints:
###Strategy

We can find the volume of the greenhouse by finding the volumes separately for the rectangular prism on bottom and the half-cylinder on top. Then, we can add those volumes together to get the total volume.

$\blueE{\text{volume}_{\text{greenhouse}}} = \maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{bottom}}} + \goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{top}}}$

###Bottom volume

The bottom is a rectangular prism whose base is $3 \times 9\text{ m}$ and height is $2\text{ m}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{prism}} &= (\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{bottom}}}  &= (3 \cdot 9) (2) \\\\
\maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{bottom}}}  &=  \maroonD{54}
\end{align}$

The volume of the bottom is $\maroonD{54 \text{ m}^3}$.

###Top volume

The top is a half cylinder with a diameter of $3\text{ m}$, so its radius is $1.5\text{ m}$. Its length (or height) is $9\text{ m}$.

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{half cylinder}} &= \dfrac12(\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\text{volume}_{\text{half cylinder}} &= \dfrac12(\pi r^2) (h) \\\\
\goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{top}}}  &= \dfrac12(\pi \cdot 1.5^2) (9) \\\\
\goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{top}}}  &=  \goldE{10.125\pi}
\end{align}$

The volume of the top is $\goldE{10.125\pi \text{ mm}^3}$.
The volume of the greenhouse is about $\blueE{86\text{ m}^3}$.

options.alt: A rectangular prism with a length of three meters, a width of nine meters, and a height of two meters. A half cylinder is on top of the prism with the same width of nine meters and the same diameter as the prism's length of three units.

items.question_content:
Rita is designing a building shaped like a pyramid that's cut off in a plane parallel to its base (this is called a truncated pyramid).



[[☃ image 1]]

The building has a $64 \times 64 \text{ m}$ square base and is $36\text{ m}$ tall. 

The cut off section has a $16 \times 16 \text{ m}$ square base and is $12\text{ m}$ tall. 

**What is the volume of the building?**  
*Round to the nearest cubic meter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}^3$
hints:
###Strategy

We can find the volume of the building by finding the volume of the entire pyramid as if there wasn't a section cut off, and then subtracting the volume of the cut off section.

$\blueE{\text{volume}_{\text{building}}} = \maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire pyramid}}} - \goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{cut off}}}$

###Entire pyramid volume

If we include the cut off section, we have a pyramid with a $64 \times 64 \text{ m}$ square base that is $48\text{ m}$ tall. 

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{pyramid}} &= \dfrac13(\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire pyramid}}}  &= \dfrac13 (64 \cdot 64) (48) \\\\
\maroonD{\text{volume}_{\text{entire pyramid}}}  &=  \maroonD{65{,}536}
\end{align}$

The entire pyramid has a volume of $\maroonD{65{,}536 \text{ m}^3}$.

###Volume of cut off section

The cut off section is a pyramid with a $16 \times 16 \text{ m}$ square base that is $12\text{ m}$ tall. 

$\begin{align}
\text{volume}_{\text{pyramid}} &= \dfrac13(\text{base area}) (\text{height}) \\\\
\goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{cut off}}}  &= \dfrac13 (16 \cdot 16) (12) \\\\
\goldE{\text{volume}_{\text{cut off}}}  &=  \goldE{1{,}024}
\end{align}$

The cut off section has a volume of $\goldE{1{,}024 \text{ m}^3}$.
The volume of the building is $\blueE{64{,}512\text{ m}^3}$.

options.alt: A square-based pyramid that has the top cut off in a plane parallel to its base. The base length of the pyramid is sixty-four meters. The height of the pyramid with the top cut off is thirty-six meters, but if the height is measured from the base to the apex, it's forty-eight meters. So, the height of the cut off part of the pyramid is twelve meters. The base length of the cut off part of the pyramid is sixteen meters.
